questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ -2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute $A + B$.
questions.explanation: Matrix addition is done entry by entry on matrices of the same size. Adding corresponding entries gives $2+4=6$, $(-1)+5=4$, $0+(-2)=-2$, and $3+1=4$, so $A+B = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 4 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$. Every position simply combines the two numbers sitting in that slot. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 8 & -5 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ comes from multiplying entrywise instead of adding.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -2 \\ 3 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute the scalar multiple $3A$.
questions.explanation: A scalar multiplies every single entry of the matrix. Multiplying each entry by $3$ gives $3\cdot1=3$, $3\cdot(-2)=-6$, $3\cdot3=9$, and $3\cdot0=0$, so $3A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -6 \\ 9 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. The zero entry stays zero because $3\cdot0=0$. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & -6 \\ 9 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ wrongly turns the $0$ into $3$, as if the scalar were added on the diagonal rather than multiplied through.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: For which sizes is the sum $A + B$ defined? Let $A$ be $2 \times 3$ and $B$ be $2 \times 3$.
options: It is defined only if $A$ and $B$ are square.
It is defined and the result is $2 \times 3$.
It is defined and the result is $3 \times 2$.
It is not defined because addition needs the inner dimensions to match.
questions.explanation: Two matrices can be added exactly when they have the same number of rows and the same number of columns, and the sum keeps those dimensions. Both $A$ and $B$ are $2 \times 3$, so the sum is defined and is itself $2 \times 3$. Addition never changes the shape; it only combines aligned entries. The trap is the inner-dimension rule, which governs matrix multiplication, not addition, so it does not apply here.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Compute the product $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: Each entry of the product is a row of the first matrix dotted with a column of the second. The entries are $1\cdot5+2\cdot7=19$, $1\cdot6+2\cdot8=22$, $3\cdot5+4\cdot7=43$, and $3\cdot6+4\cdot8=50$, giving $\begin{pmatrix} 19 & 22 \\ 43 & 50 \end{pmatrix}$. The row index of the answer entry comes from the first matrix and the column index from the second. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 12 \\ 21 & 32 \end{pmatrix}$ multiplies entrywise (the Hadamard product) instead of combining rows with columns.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$. What is the transpose $A^{T}$?
questions.explanation: The transpose turns rows into columns, so the entry in row $i$, column $j$ moves to row $j$, column $i$. The first row $(2, 1)$ becomes the first column, and the second row $(-3, 4)$ becomes the second column, giving $A^{T} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$. The diagonal entries $2$ and $4$ stay put. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ flips across the wrong (anti-)diagonal, swapping the corner entries $2$ and $4$ as well.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ and $A = \begin{pmatrix} 7 & -2 \\ 5 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$. What is $IA$?
questions.explanation: The identity matrix is the multiplicative identity, so $IA = A$ for every matrix $A$ of matching size. Carrying out the multiplication confirms it: $1\cdot7+0\cdot5=7$, $1\cdot(-2)+0\cdot6=-2$, $0\cdot7+1\cdot5=5$, $0\cdot(-2)+1\cdot6=6$, reproducing $A$ exactly. Multiplying by $I$ leaves a matrix unchanged. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 8 & -2 \\ 5 & 7 \end{pmatrix}$ comes from confusing the product $IA$ with the sum $I + A$, which would add $1$ to the diagonal.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ 5 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute $2A - B$.
questions.explanation: First scale $A$ by $2$, then subtract $B$ entry by entry. We get $2A = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$, and subtracting gives $6-1=5$, $0-4=-4$, $-2-5=-7$, $4-(-2)=6$, so $2A-B = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & -4 \\ -7 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$. Each entry is handled independently once the scaling is done. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 3 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$ forgets the subtraction signs and instead computes $2A + B$ in the off-diagonal slots.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A$ be a $2 \times 3$ matrix and $B$ be a $3 \times 2$ matrix. Which products are defined and what are their sizes?
options: Neither product is defined because the matrices differ in shape.
questions.explanation: A product $XY$ is defined when the number of columns of $X$ equals the number of rows of $Y$, and the result has the rows of $X$ and the columns of $Y$. For $AB$ the inner dimensions are $3$ and $3$, so it is defined and is $2 \times 2$; for $BA$ the inner dimensions are $2$ and $2$, so it is defined and is $3 \times 3$. Both products exist but have different shapes. The trap is assuming only one order can work, but here the inner dimensions happen to match in both orders.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 3 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. Which statement about $AB$ and $BA$ is correct?
options: $AB = BA$ because matrix multiplication is commutative.
questions.explanation: Compute both orders row-by-column. For $AB$: $1\cdot1+2\cdot3=7$, $1\cdot0+2\cdot1=2$, $0\cdot1+1\cdot3=3$, $0\cdot0+1\cdot1=1$, giving $\begin{pmatrix} 7 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. For $BA$: $1\cdot1+0\cdot0=1$, $1\cdot2+0\cdot1=2$, $3\cdot1+1\cdot0=3$, $3\cdot2+1\cdot1=7$, giving $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 7 \end{pmatrix}$. The two results differ, which shows the trap directly: matrix multiplication is not commutative, so $AB$ generally does not equal $BA$.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Find the inverse of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$ the inverse is $\tfrac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}$. Here the determinant is $2\cdot2-1\cdot3=1$, so dividing by $1$ leaves $A^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$. A quick check gives $A A^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ negates $c$ and $b$ but forgets to swap $a$ and $d$, so it places the minus signs on the wrong off-diagonal pattern.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Find the inverse of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: Using $A^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}$, the determinant is $1\cdot4-2\cdot3=-2$. The adjugate is $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -3 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, and dividing every entry by $-2$ gives $A^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ \tfrac{3}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \end{pmatrix}$. The division by the determinant is essential. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -3 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ is exactly the adjugate with the swap and sign changes done but the division by $\det A = -2$ omitted.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: For which value of $k$ does $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 3 & k \end{pmatrix}$ fail to have an inverse?
questions.explanation: A $2 \times 2$ matrix is non-invertible (singular) exactly when its determinant is zero. The determinant is $2k - 4\cdot3 = 2k - 12$, and setting $2k - 12 = 0$ gives $k = 6$. For any other $k$ the determinant is nonzero and the inverse exists. The trap option $k = 0$ comes from thinking a matrix is singular when a diagonal entry is zero, but invertibility depends on the whole determinant $2k - 12$, which vanishes only at $k = 6$.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Which matrix is symmetric (equal to its own transpose)?
questions.explanation: A matrix is symmetric when $A = A^{T}$, which for a $2 \times 2$ matrix means the two off-diagonal entries are equal. In $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ both off-diagonal entries are $3$, so reflecting across the main diagonal leaves it unchanged. The diagonal entries can be anything. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ has off-diagonal entries $2$ and $-2$, which are negatives rather than equals, so it is skew-symmetric-looking off the diagonal, not symmetric.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: A square matrix $A$ is skew-symmetric when $A^{T} = -A$. Which matrix is skew-symmetric?
questions.explanation: Skew-symmetry $A^{T} = -A$ forces each diagonal entry to satisfy $a_{ii} = -a_{ii}$, hence every diagonal entry is $0$, and the off-diagonal entries must be negatives of each other. The matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ -4 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ has zero diagonal and off-diagonal entries $4$ and $-4$, so its transpose is its negative. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 4 \\ -4 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ has the correct off-diagonal sign pattern but nonzero diagonal entries, which a skew-symmetric matrix can never have.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ -1 & 3 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute $AB$.
questions.explanation: Here $A$ is $2 \times 3$ and $B$ is $3 \times 2$, so $AB$ is $2 \times 2$ with each entry a row of $A$ dotted into a column of $B$. The entries are $1\cdot4+2\cdot0+0\cdot3=4$, $1\cdot1+2\cdot2+0\cdot(-1)=5$, $(-1)\cdot4+3\cdot0+1\cdot3=-1$, and $(-1)\cdot1+3\cdot2+1\cdot(-1)=4$. So $AB = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ -1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ -1 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$ slips on the last entry by computing $-1 + 6 = 5$ and forgetting the final $1\cdot(-1) = -1$ term of that row-column dot product.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$. Using the rule $(AB)^{T} = B^{T}A^{T}$, what is $(AB)^{T}$?
questions.explanation: First form $AB$: $1\cdot3+2\cdot2=7$, $1\cdot1+2\cdot4=9$, $0\cdot3+(-1)\cdot2=-2$, $0\cdot1+(-1)\cdot4=-4$, so $AB = \begin{pmatrix} 7 & 9 \\ -2 & -4 \end{pmatrix}$. Transposing swaps rows and columns, sending the $9$ to the lower-left slot and the $-2$ to the upper-right slot, so $(AB)^{T} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 & -2 \\ 9 & -4 \end{pmatrix}$, which also equals $B^{T}A^{T}$ by the reversal rule. The transpose is the final required step. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 7 & 9 \\ -2 & -4 \end{pmatrix}$ is $AB$ itself left untransposed.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, which expression correctly equals $(AB)^{T}$ in general?
questions.explanation: The transpose of a product reverses the order of the factors: $(AB)^{T} = B^{T}A^{T}$, never $A^{T}B^{T}$. The reversal is needed so the inner dimensions still line up after each factor is transposed. You can verify with these matrices that $B^{T}A^{T}$ reproduces the transpose of the computed product. The trap option $A^{T}B^{T}$ keeps the original order and is the single most common mistake students make with this identity.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $D = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ and $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute $DA$.
questions.explanation: Left-multiplying by a diagonal matrix scales each row of $A$ by the corresponding diagonal entry: row one by $2$ and row two by $3$. So row one $(1, 4)$ becomes $(2, 8)$ and row two $(5, 6)$ becomes $(15, 18)$, giving $DA = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 8 \\ 15 & 18 \end{pmatrix}$. You can confirm this entry by entry through the full multiplication. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 12 \\ 10 & 18 \end{pmatrix}$ scales the columns instead of the rows, which is what right-multiplication $AD$ would do, not $DA$.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ be the zero matrix and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & -1 \\ 2 & 7 \end{pmatrix}$. Which statement is correct?
questions.explanation: The zero matrix annihilates under multiplication and is neutral under addition. Multiplying any matrix by the zero matrix gives the zero matrix, so $AB = A$, while adding the zero matrix changes nothing, so $A + B = B$. These two roles are different: zero is absorbing for products but identity-like for sums. The trap option $AB = B$ confuses the zero matrix with the identity matrix, which is the one that leaves $B$ unchanged under multiplication.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 3 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$. What is $A^{T}$?
questions.explanation: Transposing a $2 \times 3$ matrix produces a $3 \times 2$ matrix whose columns are the original rows. The first row $(2, 3, -1)$ becomes the first column and the second row $(0, 1, 4)$ becomes the second column, giving $A^{T} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$. The order of entries within each row is preserved as it becomes a column. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ reverses each column top to bottom, which is not what transposition does.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Apply the elementary row operation $R_2 \to R_2 - 2R_1$ to $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$. What is the result?
questions.explanation: The operation replaces row two with row two minus twice row one, leaving row one untouched. Computing the new row two gives $2 - 2\cdot1 = 0$ and $5 - 2\cdot3 = -1$, so the matrix becomes $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$. Only the targeted row changes. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 4 & 11 \end{pmatrix}$ adds $2R_1$ to row two instead of subtracting it, getting $2 + 2 = 4$ and $5 + 6 = 11$.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Which of the following $2 \times 2$ matrices is in reduced row echelon form?
questions.explanation: Reduced row echelon form requires each leading entry (pivot) to be $1$ and to be the only nonzero entry in its column, with pivots moving right as you go down. The identity $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ meets every condition. The matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ fails because the column of the second pivot still has a nonzero $3$ above it, so it is only in row echelon form. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ has a leading entry of $2$ rather than the required $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. The matrix $A$ is best described as which special type?
questions.explanation: A matrix is upper triangular when every entry below the main diagonal is zero, which holds here since all three sub-diagonal positions are $0$. It is not diagonal, because the entry $2$ sits above the diagonal in row one, column three. It is not symmetric either, since that off-diagonal $2$ is not mirrored by a $2$ in row three, column one. The trap option lower triangular is the opposite shape: it would require the zeros above the diagonal, but here the nonzero entry is above it.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Compute the product $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: A $3 \times 3$ matrix times a $3 \times 1$ column gives a $3 \times 1$ column, each entry being a row dotted with the vector. The entries are $1\cdot1+0\cdot2+2\cdot1=3$, $0\cdot1+1\cdot2+1\cdot1=3$, and $1\cdot1+1\cdot2+0\cdot1=3$, so the product is $\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 3 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$. Each row must be fully combined with the whole column. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$ multiplies entrywise down the first column of the matrix against the vector instead of using the row-by-column rule.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ and $C = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Which sum or product is defined?
questions.explanation: Addition needs identical shapes, but $A$ is $2 \times 2$ and $C$ is $2 \times 3$, so $A + C$ is undefined. For the product $AC$, the columns of $A$ ($2$) match the rows of $C$ ($2$), so $AC$ is defined and inherits the rows of $A$ and the columns of $C$, making it $2 \times 3$. The order $CA$ fails because the columns of $C$ ($3$) do not match the rows of $A$ ($2$). The trap option $A + C$ ignores that addition requires conformable, equal-sized matrices.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute $A^{2}$.
questions.explanation: The power $A^{2}$ means the matrix product $A \cdot A$, computed row-by-column, not each entry squared. The entries are $1\cdot1+2\cdot0=1$, $1\cdot2+2\cdot1=4$, $0\cdot1+1\cdot0=0$, and $0\cdot2+1\cdot1=1$, giving $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. The off-diagonal entry grows to $4$ because of the cross terms in the dot products. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ squares each diagonal entry separately, turning the lower-right $1$ into $1$ but the reasoning into entrywise squaring rather than true matrix multiplication.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ -2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute $AB - BA$ to test commutativity.
questions.explanation: Compute each product separately and then subtract. $AB = \begin{pmatrix} 2\cdot0+(-1)(-2) & 2\cdot4+(-1)\cdot1 \\ 1\cdot0+3\cdot(-2) & 1\cdot4+3\cdot1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 7 \\ -6 & 7 \end{pmatrix}$ and $BA = \begin{pmatrix} 0\cdot2+4\cdot1 & 0\cdot(-1)+4\cdot3 \\ -2\cdot2+1\cdot1 & -2\cdot(-1)+1\cdot3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 12 \\ -3 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$. Then $AB - BA = \begin{pmatrix} 2-4 & 7-12 \\ -6-(-3) & 7-5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & -5 \\ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, which is nonzero, so $AB \neq BA$. The trap option is the zero matrix, which would wrongly assume matrix multiplication is commutative.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$. Solve $AX = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ for the column vector $X$ using $A^{-1}$.
questions.explanation: Since $\det A = 3\cdot2 - 5\cdot1 = 1$, the inverse is $A^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -5 \\ -1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ with no division needed. Then $X = A^{-1}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2\cdot1+(-5)\cdot1 \\ -1\cdot1+3\cdot1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$. Substituting back, $A\begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -9+10 \\ -3+4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$, confirming the solution. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$ negates the correct answer by forming the adjugate without the swap of the diagonal entries.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Compute the $3 \times 3$ product $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: Left-multiplying by a diagonal matrix scales each row of the second matrix by the matching diagonal entry. Row one is scaled by $1$, row two by $2$ giving $(8, 10, 12)$, and row three by $3$ giving $(21, 24, 27)$, so the product is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 8 & 10 & 12 \\ 21 & 24 & 27 \end{pmatrix}$. The diagonal entries act on whole rows, not on individual diagonal positions. The trap option $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 4 & 9 \\ 4 & 10 & 18 \\ 7 & 16 & 27 \end{pmatrix}$ multiplies the two matrices entrywise instead of using the diagonal-scaling (row) rule.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Suppose $A$ and $B$ are symmetric $n \times n$ matrices. Under what condition is the product $AB$ guaranteed to be symmetric?
options: $AB$ is always symmetric because $A$ and $B$ are symmetric.
$AB$ is symmetric if and only if $A$ and $B$ commute, that is $AB = BA$.
$AB$ is symmetric only when both are diagonal.
questions.explanation: Using the reversal rule, $(AB)^{T} = B^{T}A^{T} = BA$ because $A$ and $B$ equal their own transposes. So $AB$ is symmetric exactly when $(AB)^{T} = AB$, which means $BA = AB$, the commuting condition. Symmetry of the factors alone is not enough; the order matters because multiplication is generally noncommutative. The trap option claims $AB$ is always symmetric, forgetting that $(AB)^{T} = B^{T}A^{T}$ reverses the factors and leaves $BA$, not $AB$.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ -2 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute $A + B$.
questions.solution: Before combining anything, check legality: the sum $A + B$ is defined exactly when {{1}}. The sum is then formed by {{2}}. Working through the four positions, the bottom-left entry of $A + B$ equals {{3}}, and the completed sum is {{4}}.

options: the two matrices have the same number of rows and the same number of columns
the number of columns of $A$ matches the number of rows of $B$
gaps.answer: the two matrices have the same number of rows and the same number of columns

options: adding the entries that occupy the same position
dotting each row of $A$ with each column of $B$
multiplying the entries that occupy the same position
adding each row of $A$ to each column of $B$
gaps.answer: adding the entries that occupy the same position

questions.explanation: Addition is legal only for matrices of identical size: same row count and same column count. The inner-dimension test (columns of $A$ against rows of $B$) belongs to matrix multiplication, and neither squareness nor invertibility has anything to do with sums. Once the shapes match, each position adds independently: $2 + 5 = 7$, $-3 + 0 = -3$, $1 + (-2) = -1$, and $4 + 6 = 10$, so $A + B = \begin{pmatrix} 7 & -3 \\ -1 & 10 \end{pmatrix}$. The option $\begin{pmatrix} 10 & 0 \\ -2 & 24 \end{pmatrix}$ multiplies entrywise instead of adding, $\begin{pmatrix} -3 & -3 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$ computes $A - B$, and $\begin{pmatrix} 7 & -3 \\ 3 & 10 \end{pmatrix}$ slips into subtraction in the bottom-left slot, turning $1 + (-2)$ into $1 - (-2)$.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -2 \\ 4 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute the scalar multiple $3A$.
questions.solution: Written without a scalar, $3A$ unpacks to {{1}}. That expansion shows the factor $3$ reaches {{2}}. The second row of $3A$ is therefore {{3}}, and the top-right entry works out to {{4}}.

options: the matrix with $3$ added to each entry
only the entries of one row

questions.explanation: Scalar multiplication is repeated addition: $3A = A + A + A$, so every entry of $A$ picks up its own factor of $3$. It is $A + 3I$ that would touch only the diagonal, adding $3$ to each entry is a different operation altogether, and $A^{3}$ is a triple matrix product, not a scalar multiple. Tripling the second row $(4, 0)$ gives $(12, 0)$: the zero entry stays $0$ because $3 \cdot 0 = 0$. The row $(12, 3)$ turns the zero into a $3$ as if the scalar were added there, $(7, 3)$ adds $3$ to both entries, and $(4, 0)$ leaves the row untouched as if the scalar skipped it. Finally the top-right entry is $3 \cdot (-2) = -6$, so $3A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -6 \\ 12 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Compute the product $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ 2 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.solution: Each entry of a matrix product comes from {{1}}. In particular, the bottom-left entry is assembled as {{2}}, which evaluates to {{3}}. Carrying out the same recipe in all four positions gives the product {{4}}.

options: a row of the first factor dotted with a column of the second
a column of the first factor dotted with a row of the second
the two entries in that position multiplied together
the two entries in that position added together
gaps.answer: a row of the first factor dotted with a column of the second

questions.explanation: Matrix multiplication is row times column: the entry in row $i$, column $j$ dots row $i$ of the left factor with column $j$ of the right factor. The bottom-left entry therefore uses row $2$ of the left matrix, $(0, 3)$, and column $1$ of the right matrix, $(1, 2)$: $0 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 2 = 6$. Choosing $0 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot (-1)$ dots row $2$ with the wrong column, $2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2$ dots the wrong row with column $1$, and $0 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 4$ pairs a row with a row, which is not how the product works. Repeating the rule everywhere gives $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 7 \\ 6 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$. The option $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 0 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$ multiplies entrywise, $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 13 \\ 4 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ is the reversed product $BA$, a reminder that order matters, and $\begin{pmatrix} 6 & 3 \\ 12 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$ comes from pairing rows with rows throughout.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 5 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$. Describe and compute its transpose $A^{T}$.
questions.solution: Transposing turns each row of $A$ into the corresponding column of $A^{T}$, so here $A^{T}$ has size {{1}}. Chasing a single entry: the $-1$ sitting in row $1$, column $2$ of $A$ lands in {{2}} of $A^{T}$. The complete second row of $A^{T}$ is {{3}}, and the entry in row $3$, column $2$ of $A^{T}$ equals {{4}}.
questions.explanation: A $2 \times 3$ matrix transposes to a $3 \times 2$ matrix: the two rows of $A$ become the two columns of $A^{T}$. Keeping the size at $2 \times 3$ forgets that the roles of rows and columns swap. Entry by entry the rule is $A^{T}_{ij} = A_{ji}$, so the $-1$ at row $1$, column $2$ moves to row $2$, column $1$; leaving it at row $1$, column $2$ treats the transpose as doing nothing, and row $2$, column $2$ reflects across the wrong diagonal. The second row of $A^{T}$ is the second column of $A$ read top to bottom, $(-1, 5)$: the option $(0, 5, 4)$ keeps a row of $A$ intact, $(5, -1)$ reverses the order of the column, and $(3, 0)$ grabs the first column instead. Finally $A^{T}_{32} = A_{23} = 4$, completing $A^{T} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ -1 & 5 \\ 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Find the inverse of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.solution: Writing $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$, an inverse exists exactly when {{1}}. For this matrix the test quantity comes out to {{2}}, so the inverse exists. The formula divides the adjugate by the determinant, and the adjugate is built as {{3}}. Putting the pieces together, $A^{-1} =$ {{4}}.
questions.explanation: Invertibility of a $2 \times 2$ matrix is governed by the determinant $ad - bc$: the inverse exists exactly when it is nonzero. It does not need to equal $1$ or be positive, and zero entries elsewhere in a matrix are harmless. Here $\det A = 4 \cdot 1 - 3 \cdot 1 = 1$: adding the products gives $7$, reversing the subtraction gives $-1$, and $4$ uses the main diagonal alone. The adjugate swaps the diagonal entries and negates the off-diagonal ones, $\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}$; negating without swapping, or swapping the off-diagonal pair instead, are the standard slips. Dividing by $\det A = 1$ changes nothing, so $A^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -3 \\ -1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$. The option $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ swaps but forgets the minus signs, $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ negates but forgets the swap, and $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ is the transpose, which is not the inverse. A quick check confirms $A A^{-1} = I$.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: For which value of $k$ does $A = \begin{pmatrix} k & 6 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ fail to have an inverse?
questions.solution: A square matrix fails to have an inverse exactly when {{1}}. In terms of $k$, the relevant quantity for this matrix is {{2}}. Setting it equal to zero and solving yields $k =$ {{3}}. At that value the failure can be seen directly in the rows, because {{4}}.

options: its two diagonal entries are equal
one row becomes a multiple of the other
gaps.answer: one row becomes a multiple of the other

questions.explanation: Having no inverse means being singular, and singular is exactly determinant zero. A negative determinant is perfectly invertible, a zero entry proves nothing, and the diagonal entries carry no special role here. By $ad - bc$ the determinant is $k \cdot 3 - 6 \cdot 2 = 3k - 12$: the option $3k + 12$ adds the anti-diagonal product instead of subtracting it, while $2k - 18$ and $6k - 6$ pair the entries across the wrong diagonals. Solving $3k - 12 = 0$ gives $k = 4$. At that value the first row $(4, 6)$ is exactly twice the second row $(2, 3)$, so the rows are proportional and hence linearly dependent, which is what a zero determinant records. Neither row is zero, the rows are not equal to each other, and the matrix is nowhere near the identity.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute $A^{2}$.
questions.solution: The power $A^{2}$ means {{1}}. Under that reading, the top-right entry of $A^{2}$ is assembled as {{2}}, which evaluates to {{3}}. Completing the other entries the same way gives $A^{2} =$ {{4}}.

options: the matrix whose entries are the squares of the entries of $A$

questions.explanation: The power $A^{2}$ is the genuine matrix product $A \cdot A$, computed row times column; it is not entrywise squaring, not the scalar multiple $2A$, and not $A A^{T}$. The top-right entry dots row $1$ of $A$, which is $(2, -1)$, with column $2$ of $A$, which is $(-1, 3)$: $2 \cdot (-1) + (-1) \cdot 3 = -2 - 3 = -5$. Squaring the single entry gives $(-1)(-1) = 1$, pairing row $1$ with row $2$ gives $2 \cdot 0 + (-1) \cdot 3 = -3$, and $2 \cdot 2 + (-1) \cdot 0$ builds the top-left entry, not the top-right one. Assembling all four entries, $A^{2} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -5 \\ 0 & 9 \end{pmatrix}$; the bottom-left stays $0$ because a product of upper triangular matrices is upper triangular. The option with $1$ in the top-right squares each entry separately, $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ 0 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$ is $2A$, and $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ 0 & 9 \end{pmatrix}$ keeps only the first term of the top-right dot product.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra

questions.stem: For which value of $x$ is the matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & x \\ 7 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ symmetric?
questions.solution: Symmetry means $A = A^{T}$, and for a $2 \times 2$ matrix that reduces to the single requirement that {{1}}. Imposing it on this matrix forces $x =$ {{2}}. One step further: could some other choice of $x$ make this matrix skew-symmetric instead? The answer is {{3}}.

options: the two off-diagonal entries are equal
the two off-diagonal entries are negatives of each other
the two diagonal entries are equal
gaps.answer: the two off-diagonal entries are equal

options: no, because skew-symmetry forces the diagonal entries to be $0$ and here they are $3$
yes, $x = -7$ makes the off-diagonal pair skew, which is all that is needed
yes, the same $x$ that makes it symmetric also makes it skew-symmetric
yes, any $x$ works because the transpose keeps the diagonal fixed
gaps.answer: no, because skew-symmetry forces the diagonal entries to be $0$ and here they are $3$

questions.explanation: For a $2 \times 2$ matrix, $A = A^{T}$ says the entry above the diagonal equals the entry below it: $A_{12} = A_{21}$. Requiring negatives of each other is the skew-symmetry condition, and the diagonal entries of a symmetric matrix are free, so neither of those alternatives is the right test. Matching $x$ with the $7$ below the diagonal gives $x = 7$, and the matrix then reflects onto itself across the main diagonal. Skew-symmetry, by contrast, is impossible here for every $x$: the condition $A^{T} = -A$ forces each diagonal entry to satisfy $a = -a$, hence to be $0$, but this matrix carries $3$ in both diagonal slots. Choosing $x = -7$ does fix the off-diagonal pair, yet the nonzero diagonal still violates the definition, so the off-diagonal condition alone is not enough.
questions.source: in the style of Leon, Linear Algebra with Applications

questions.stem: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$. Use the transpose rule to find $(AB)^{T}$.
questions.solution: The transpose of a product obeys $(AB)^{T} =$ {{1}}. To pin down actual numbers, build one entry of $AB$ first: its top-right entry is assembled as {{2}}, which equals {{3}}. After transposing, that value sits in {{4}} of $(AB)^{T}$.

options: $AB$ itself, because transposing a product changes nothing

questions.explanation: Transposing a product reverses the factors: $(AB)^{T} = B^{T}A^{T}$. Keeping the order as $A^{T}B^{T}$ is the classic error; the reversal is what keeps the inner dimensions aligned once each factor is transposed. The sum rule $A^{T} + B^{T}$ belongs to $(A + B)^{T}$, and transposing certainly changes a product whenever the result is not symmetric. Concretely, the top-right entry of $AB$ dots row $1$ of $A$, which is $(1, 2)$, with column $2$ of $B$, which is $(0, 3)$: $1 \cdot 0 + 2 \cdot 3 = 6$. The option $1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 1$ builds the top-left entry instead, $1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 3$ pairs row $1$ of $A$ with row $2$ of $B$, and $0 \cdot 0 + (-1) \cdot 3$ builds the bottom-right entry. Transposition swaps row and column indices, so the value at row $1$, column $2$ of $AB$ moves to row $2$, column $1$ of $(AB)^{T}$. In full, $AB = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ -1 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$ and $(AB)^{T} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 \\ 6 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$, which matches $B^{T}A^{T}$.
questions.source: in the style of Anton, Elementary Linear Algebra
